segments.text: But the problem here is basically because the loss,
you're calculating the likelihood of the correct answer.
And the likelihood of the correct answer
is the probability of getting the correct answer.
The accuracy is the number of times
you're getting the correct answer.
So as you train a model to get more and more confident,
it gets better and better at getting more answers correct.
But it also gets more and more confident in its answers.
And so if there's any example that it's really bad at,
it might get very confident in that answer,
that bad answer.
And the log likelihood of that answer will go up.
Or sorry, the log likelihood will go down.
So the negative log likelihood will go up.
It is the loss.
So basically,
the loss that you're calculating
and the thing that you care about in the end,
accuracy can be decorrelated.
So there is also an interesting example in text generation.
And this is part of the reason why we have
all these other text generation algorithms
like nucleosamplating, their top case sampling
or other things like this is actually,
in a maximum likelihood trained model,
better search,
in other words, finding a better model score
doesn't necessarily give you a better generation result.
And this is an example from machine translation
from a really long time ago.
But it's still persists today,
which is they did beam search with a larger and larger beam.
And the best beam for finding the best scoring output,
basically was four.
And then the accuracy goes down and down and down
as they find a better output.
And does anyone remember when we talked about
the generation class where this comes from?
I don't know how explicitly we mentioned it
in the generation class.
But basically, the problem is,
maximum likelihood trained models like shorter outputs,
generally because if we make the output longer,
the probability of the longer outputs goes down.
So as you'll be, it will start generating shorter
and shorter outputs.
And because of that, the score goes down
because blue score doesn't like outputs
that are too short, essentially.
So there are effects around this for beam search,
where essentially what you do is you take the average
log likelihood of each token instead of the overall
log likelihood of each token.
And that improves a little bit,
but still you can see as you search more
of the accuracy goes down.
So that's the general idea here.
There's a bunch of ways you can fix this.
The most principled way is to use a method
like reinforcement learning or something,
some sort of structured training algorithm
that allows you to train your models
so that you don't get these bad outputs.
Another way that's much easier is to do early stopping
with the evaluation metric is opposed
to early stopping with the loss.
And by doing this, you would stop here.
So you would stop where you get the highest evaluation metric
that you care about instead of stopping here.
So that's one way you can fix this problem.
Does anyone have an idea about why this might be a bad idea?
Why might it be a bad idea to stop here
instead of stopping here?
I think.
Yeah.
So it's kind of overfitting.
It's overfitting in a particular way.
But remember, here, this is still the accuracy on the dev set.
So we're not overfitting so much
that the accuracy is going down.
That would be a different variety of overfitting.
But any ideas?
Go for it.
We don't want it to be too confident in the volume.
Yeah, exactly.
We don't want it to be too confident in its wrong answers.
So we talked about calibration, where calibration
is basically like how accurate are the probability estimates.
So this model over here is going to be really poorly calibrated.
It's going to be very confident regardless
of whether it's correct or not.
And that could be a problem in downstream tasks.
There's also another thing that I forgot to put on.
That I forgot to put on the slides,
but it's an interesting phenomenon that actually kind of a lot
of people in interpretability are interested in.
It's this generalization, gracking,
generalization beyond overfitting on small algorithmic data
sets.
And basically what they show is you can
be training for a very, very long time.
And reducing the loss, reducing the loss,
reducing the loss, and reducing the loss.
And it's only after a very long time,
does your model start generalizing well
and getting good at accuracy?
Though this paper, the types of data sets
it's talking about are data sets where
you need to get many things in a row correct
before you get the final answer correct.
So basically you need to get 20 steps in a row
or 50 steps in a row correct before you get the final answer
correct.
And basically the reason why this happens
is because this accuracy will keep going up.
But you only get the accuracy of each individual decision
will keep going up.
But you only get marked correct after you get all 50 in a row
correct.
So this difference can be even more stark when you're
talking about things that require like 50 steps of reasoning
or multiple steps of reasoning, but like 50 token
generations is correct before you get them right.
So that's another thing to be aware of.
Cool.
So now I want to switch gears a little bit
to actionable evaluation and how you can evaluate your models
in a way that makes it easy to find next steps
to be improving.
Are there any questions about the debugging part
before we get into this part?
OK.
It's not over.
So my first suggestion with respect
to how you can actually improve systems
is make sure that you're looking at the data that you're using.
And both bugs and new research directions
can be found by looking at your model outputs.
So to give one example of a very common mistake
that you can make when you're creating a generation algorithm,
it's the sort of off by one errors.
So let's say you implemented a translation system.
And it's generating outputs like went to the story
yesterday, bought a dog.
You can immediately look at this and say, hey,
this doesn't look like natural English.
What's going on?
And the problem here is you're doing something
like outputs one.
And you have a slice of one instead of zero here or something
like this.
So this is a really silly error that you might just
make a mistake on Python and your preprocessing
or post-processing or something like this.
But the problem is if you look at your blue score based
evaluation or something like that,
you'll be one point worse or two points worse
or something like that.
And you'll be like, oh, I'm two points worse.
Why am I two points worse than the state of the art?
And it turns out it was a really silly thing like this.
And immediately you'll see this if you look at your data.
But if you're doing all your experiments
and just looking at the numbers, it's really hard
to tell why this is happening.
Another thing is if you have a good eye
and can just look through the data points,
we as humans are pretty good pattern recognizers.
And especially CMU students,
you're very good and quick at picking up on things.
So if you look at the data and pull through things,
you can probably pick up patterns about why things are failing.
And so you might look and see that compared to some other model,
your model is really bad at answering questions
about people or something like that.
And then you figure out you'll need a better model of people
or your Rags system that you're building for assignment
too is maybe failing on all the research-related questions.
So you need to come up with the research,
like scrape more research data or something like that.
So there are methods to do this more systematically.
